heroOverview: Hypercementosis is the excessive deposition of cementum on one or more tooth roots beyond their normal contour, producing characteristically bulbous or club-shaped root ends visible on radiographic examination. The condition affects an estimated 1–5% of the general population, though prevalence is substantially higher — up to 50–60% — in patients with Paget disease of bone. Excess cementum forms because cementoblasts, the cells that normally maintain the root surface, remain inappropriately active after root completion, sometimes in response to chronic periapical inflammation, occlusal overloading, or systemic metabolic disease. Hypercementosis itself is almost always asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on dental radiographs. Its clinical importance lies principally in the mechanical difficulty it creates during tooth extraction, where the bulbous root may anchor itself in the socket, and in its role as a radiographic marker of underlying systemic conditions warranting investigation.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 1–5% of adults in the general population; prevalence reaches 30–60% in Paget disease of bone patients
keyStats.demographics: More common in older adults; prevalence increases progressively with age; no strong sex predominance in idiopathic cases
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly identified in adults aged 40–70 during routine dental radiographic surveys
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global case counts are not established; hypercementosis is reported worldwide across all populations

definition: Hypercementosis (ICD-10: K03.4) is a non-neoplastic, idiopathic or secondary excessive proliferation of cementum on the surface of one or more tooth roots, beyond the cementoenamel junction and the normal root contour. Cementum is the mineralised connective tissue that covers the anatomical root, functions as the attachment site for the periodontal ligament fibres, and is continuously remodelled by cementoblasts throughout life. In hypercementosis, cementoblast activity exceeds resorption, producing localised or generalised root enlargement. The process may be limited to the apical third of the root (the most common pattern, producing a round apical bulb), may extend over the entire root, or, rarely, may cause roots of adjacent teeth to fuse (concrescence). The condition is broadly classified as generalised hypercementosis — when most or all teeth in both jaws are affected, strongly raising concern for systemic disease — or localised, when it is limited to one or a few teeth, usually in the setting of chronic apical or periodontal inflammation or occlusally overloaded teeth. Histologically, the excess cementum may be cellular (containing cementocyte lacunae) or acellular, and is typically deposited in concentric layers visible as an irregular outer root contour on periapical or panoramic radiographs. The periodontal ligament space often appears compressed or absent radiographically at the hypercementotic apex.

typesClassification.description: Excess cementum on one or a few teeth, typically in response to local stimuli: chronic low-grade periapical inflammation, excessive occlusal loading, orthodontic forces, or a retained primary tooth forcing the permanent successor into altered function. The maxillary premolars and mandibular first molars are most often affected.

Involves most or all teeth in both dental arches. Strongly associated with systemic metabolic disorders — principally Paget disease of bone, acromegaly, and hyperparathyroidism — where aberrant calcium-phosphate metabolism drives excess cementum production. The degree of root enlargement can be severe in Paget disease, occasionally causing ankylosis.

An extreme form where hypercementosis causes the roots of two adjacent teeth to fuse via their cementum, effectively joining the teeth into a single unit. This complicates both extraction and orthodontic movement.

Localised nodular deposits of cementum protruding from the root surface (cementicles) rather than diffuse root enlargement. Often found in older adults and associated with periodontal disease or periapical pathology.

primarySymptoms: Absence of symptoms in the vast majority of cases — hypercementosis is most often discovered incidentally on routine dental periapical or panoramic radiographs, with no pain, swelling, or functional complaint.
Radiographic finding of a bulbous, club-shaped, or rounded root apex, often with a thick periodontal ligament space at the apex that may appear compressed, detected by a dentist during routine care.
Tooth immobility — in severe cases the hypercementotic root becomes ankylosed to the surrounding alveolar bone, making the tooth appear more firmly fixed than adjacent teeth.
Difficulty or inability to exfoliate a primary (baby) tooth when hypercementosis has occurred, delaying eruption of the underlying permanent tooth.
Resistance or difficulty during tooth extraction, when the bulbous root locks within the bony socket walls, requiring surgical removal rather than simple forceps delivery.
Bone pain or deformity of the jaw in the rare setting of severe generalised Paget disease — though this is a symptom of the underlying Paget disease rather than of hypercementosis itself.
Dull ache or tenderness at the root tip in a small subset of cases with associated chronic periapical periodontitis, where the hypercementosis formed in response to the periapical inflammation.
Slight biting sensitivity when the affected tooth also has associated periapical pathology contributing to the hypercementosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Routine dental radiograph showing an unusually rounded or thick root apex in the absence of symptoms — report to a dentist for evaluation of a possible systemic cause.
Generalised jaw aching and bone enlargement alongside multiple root changes on X-ray, which may indicate Paget disease of bone.
Jaw swelling, facial deformity, or multiple extraction failures prompting referral for specialist dental imaging and systemic metabolic evaluation.
emergencySigns: Severe swelling of the jaw or face following a complicated tooth extraction, suggesting retained root fragments or alveolar fracture during removal of an ankylosed hypercementotic root.
Signs of Paget disease complications — sudden hearing loss, nerve compression symptoms, or pathological fracture — in a patient known to have generalised hypercementosis.
Acute signs of hypercalcaemia (confusion, extreme thirst, nausea, abdominal pain) in a patient with generalised hypercementosis prompting urgent blood chemistry testing.

causes.description: The most important systemic cause of generalised hypercementosis; in Paget disease, the normal cycle of bone and cementum resorption and formation is dysregulated, leading to excessive, architecturally disordered cementum on tooth roots across the entire dentition, sometimes causing ankylosis. Prevalence of hypercementosis in Paget disease reaches 30–60%.

Long-standing periapical periodontitis — from pulp necrosis, a carious exposure, or a failed root canal — sustains low-grade irritation of the apical periodontal ligament and cementoblasts. This chronic inflammatory stimulus drives reactive cementum deposition around the apex as a walling-off response, producing the localised apical bulb pattern.

Teeth subjected to abnormally heavy biting forces — from parafunctional habits (bruxism, clenching), unopposed teeth, or teeth that have taken over the function of missing ones — respond with increased cementum deposition at the apical region as an adaptive thickening of the periodontal attachment apparatus.

Primary hyperparathyroidism and acromegaly both alter calcium-phosphate metabolism and growth hormone signalling pathways that influence mineralising cell activity, including cementoblasts. Generalised root hypercementosis is a recognised dental finding in these endocrine disorders, though less prominent than in Paget disease.

causes.cause: Retained primary teeth and delayed eruption
causes.description: When a primary tooth is retained beyond its normal exfoliation time — because the permanent successor is absent, impacted, or displaced — prolonged loading and inflammatory stimuli act on the primary root, driving hypercementosis that further anchors the retained tooth and complicates extraction.

riskFactors.description: The single strongest association with generalised hypercementosis; up to 60% of patients with active Paget disease affecting the jaw develop radiographically visible root enlargement, often severe enough to cause ankylosis.

Hypercementosis prevalence increases steadily with age as cumulative cementum deposition over decades of root loading and minor periapical events accumulates; most affected individuals are in their fifth decade or beyond.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic dental disease (periapical periodontitis, untreated caries)
riskFactors.description: Pulp necrosis and resulting chronic periapical inflammation are the most modifiable local causes of hypercementosis; prompt restorative and endodontic treatment reduces the chronic inflammatory stimulus that drives reactive cementum deposition.

Both primary and secondary hyperparathyroidism alter the mineral homeostasis that governs cementoblast activity; control of hyperparathyroidism through parathyroidectomy or medical management may reduce further cementum accumulation.
Heavy occlusal parafunctional loading chronically stimulates adaptive root changes including cementum thickening; use of an occlusal splint or other bruxism management may reduce this stimulus.

riskFactors.factor: Retained primary teeth without permanent successors (hypodontia)
riskFactors.description: Primary teeth retained into adulthood due to congenital absence of permanent successors are subject to prolonged loading and gradual hypercementosis, contributing to their eventual ankylosis.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40–70 in whom decades of cementum deposition become radiographically evident
Patients with diagnosed Paget disease of bone, the strongest systemic association
Individuals with untreated chronic periapical periodontitis or long-standing dental caries
People with primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism or acromegaly
Patients with retained primary teeth beyond the normal exfoliation age
Individuals with bruxism or parafunctional occlusal habits
diagnosisOverview: Hypercementosis is a radiographic diagnosis. The characteristic appearance is a rounded or bulbous root apex with a preserved but sometimes irregular periodontal ligament space, clearly distinct from the normal root taper. Periapical radiographs provide the highest-resolution view of individual roots and are the primary imaging tool; panoramic radiography is useful for surveying generalised involvement across the full dentition. Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is reserved for surgical planning — specifically to map root morphology and proximity to anatomical structures before tooth extraction or implant placement in hypercementotic sites. The diagnosis should prompt investigation of a systemic cause when hypercementosis is generalised (involving multiple teeth in both jaws). Blood tests include serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and parathyroid hormone (PTH) to screen for Paget disease (markedly elevated ALP), hyperparathyroidism (elevated PTH, elevated serum calcium), and other metabolic bone diseases. Serum bone-specific ALP and urinary deoxypyridinoline are more sensitive markers for active Paget disease activity. The radiographic differential diagnosis includes periapical condensing osteitis (radio-opacity around the root tip but within the bone rather than on the root), cementoblastoma (a benign tumour fused to a specific root with a distinct radiopaque mass and a radiolucent rim), and normal anatomical root anatomy on an oblique projection. A cementoblastoma must be actively excluded because it requires surgical removal, whereas hypercementosis is managed conservatively unless extraction is necessary.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging — provides a high-resolution view of the affected root, showing the characteristic bulbous root contour, the periodontal ligament space width, and distinguishing hypercementosis from periapical bone lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small film or digital sensor is placed inside the mouth against the tooth; a focused X-ray is taken from outside in under a second. The dentist examines the image immediately. The radiation dose is very low.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Surveys all teeth and their roots in a single image to assess for generalised hypercementosis across the dentition and to screen for other jaw pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You bite on a small bite peg while a rotating arm circles your head over about 15 seconds. Completely painless and involving very low X-ray exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides three-dimensional root and bone anatomy before tooth extraction in a hypercementotic root, enabling the surgeon to plan the approach and anticipate which bone must be removed to deliver the tooth without fracture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Seated in a chair with a chin rest while the machine rotates around your head for 10–40 seconds, taking multiple images. Higher dose than a panoramic film but far lower than a medical CT scan.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP), calcium, and phosphate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for Paget disease of bone (markedly elevated ALP) and hyperparathyroidism (elevated calcium, low phosphate, elevated PTH) when generalised hypercementosis is found on radiographs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from a vein in the arm; results are usually available within 24 hours. Bone-specific ALP adds specificity for skeletal disease if total ALP is elevated due to liver causes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes hyperparathyroidism when elevated serum calcium accompanies generalised hypercementosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw processed by the laboratory; PTH is labile so samples are processed quickly on ice or in a chilled tube.

treatmentOverview: Because hypercementosis itself is almost always asymptomatic, it rarely requires specific treatment beyond observation and radiographic monitoring. The appropriate response depends entirely on whether an underlying systemic cause is identified and on whether the affected tooth requires intervention for other dental reasons. When hypercementosis is an incidental finding on a healthy tooth with no associated pathology, no treatment is indicated; the patient is informed of the finding and surveillance continues as part of routine dental care. If the tooth has associated chronic periapical periodontitis — which commonly drives localised hypercementosis — root canal therapy is the definitive management: it eliminates the bacterial source driving the apical inflammation, arrests further cementum deposition, and restores the tooth to function. The hypercementosis itself does not reverse, but progression ceases after the inflammatory stimulus is removed. When a hypercementotic tooth requires extraction — because of irreparable damage, periodontal breakdown, or severe caries — the bulbous root morphology demands a surgical extraction rather than a simple forceps approach. The dentist or oral surgeon divides the root or removes surrounding bone to deliver the tooth in sections, avoiding the jaw fracture or root fracture that can result from attempting to force a bulbous root out of an unyielding socket. When generalised hypercementosis is found alongside Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism, the systemic condition is the medical priority: treatment with bisphosphonates for Paget disease reduces disease activity and may slow further skeletal and cementum changes. Hyperparathyroidism is managed by parathyroidectomy or medical therapy depending on the severity.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation and radiographic monitoring
medicalTreatments.description: The standard management for asymptomatic incidental hypercementosis on otherwise healthy teeth; annual or biennial periapical radiographs confirm that the finding is stable and no periapical pathology develops.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate monitoring prevents missed development of superimposed periapical disease in approximately 95% of stable cases; no numerical efficacy applies to observation itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Root canal therapy (endodontic treatment)
medicalTreatments.description: When hypercementosis is associated with periapical periodontitis from pulp necrosis or chronic infection, root canal treatment removes the inflammatory stimulus. Chemomechanical cleaning and obturation of the root canal system arrest further cementum deposition and heal the periapical lesion in 75–90% of cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Periapical healing after root canal therapy occurs in approximately 80–90% of cases with initial periapical lesion when performed by a specialist; 5-year success rates above 85% in well-controlled studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonate therapy for Paget disease
medicalTreatments.description: Zoledronic acid (single intravenous infusion) or oral risedronate normalises bone turnover in Paget disease, reducing ALP to near-normal levels in 90% of treated patients and potentially slowing the cementum changes that underlie generalised hypercementosis in Paget disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV achieves biochemical remission in approximately 90% of patients with Paget disease at 6 months versus 74% for risedronate in comparative trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Parathyroidectomy or medical management for hyperparathyroidism
medicalTreatments.description: Primary hyperparathyroidism causing generalised hypercementosis is treated by surgical removal of the overactive parathyroid gland or glands, normalising PTH and calcium, which removes the systemic drive for excess mineralising cell activity including cementoblasts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parathyroidectomy achieves biochemical cure in 95–98% of patients with single-gland adenoma; post-operative ALP normalises and bone disease stabilises in the great majority.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical (open) tooth extraction
surgicalOptions.description: When a hypercementotic tooth must be removed, a surgical approach is used: the gum is reflected to expose the socket, bone is removed with a drill to access the bulbous root, the root is sectioned vertically to reduce its diameter, and the sections are delivered individually. This prevents jaw fracture and root retention.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical extraction achieves complete root removal in approximately 95% of cases without serious complications when performed by an experienced oral surgeon using pre-operative CBCT guidance.

surgicalOptions.name: Periapical surgery (apicectomy) with root-end resection
surgicalOptions.description: If root canal therapy does not resolve periapical disease associated with hypercementosis — because the apical anatomy is complex — surgical access through the gum and bone to the root tip allows resection of the hypercementotic apex and retrograde sealing of the root canal. Reserved for cases where conventional retreatment fails.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates of approximately 80–90% at 4 years in modern microsurgical apicectomy series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occlusal splint for bruxism-related hypercementosis
alternativeTreatments.description: A custom-made hard acrylic mouth guard worn at night redistributes occlusal forces and reduces parafunctional loading on teeth with hypercementosis from hyperfunction, potentially slowing further cementum deposition in this subtype.

alternativeTreatments.name: Elimination of local irritants
alternativeTreatments.description: Removal of defective restorations with overhanging margins, treatment of gingival inflammation, and correction of traumatic occlusal contacts reduce the local stimuli that perpetuate localised reactive hypercementosis.

whySeeSpecialist: Most patients with incidental hypercementosis on routine dental X-rays do not need specialist referral — their general dentist can monitor the finding and treat any associated periapical disease. Referral to an oral and maxillofacial surgeon or endodontist is appropriate when the tooth requires extraction (to plan a surgical approach with CBCT guidance) or when root canal retreatment of a complex apical case is needed. When generalised hypercementosis is found — involving many teeth in both jaws — referral to a physician for metabolic bone disease assessment is essential, as this pattern is the radiographic signature of Paget disease or a significant endocrine disorder.
doctorSelectionGuide: For routine hypercementosis, continue care with your general dentist who can monitor radiographically and treat any associated dental disease. For complex extraction or endodontic retreatment, seek an oral and maxillofacial surgeon or endodontist familiar with the added surgical complexity of bulbous root morphology. If a systemic cause is identified, care is coordinated with a rheumatologist or endocrinologist for Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism management. Ask the oral surgeon whether they routinely obtain CBCT before extracting hypercementotic teeth.
preventionStrategies: Attend regular dental check-ups and treat dental caries and gum disease promptly to prevent the chronic periapical inflammation that drives localised reactive hypercementosis.
If Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism is diagnosed, inform your dentist; systemic treatment reduces disease activity and may slow further changes in the jaw.
Manage bruxism with an occlusal splint, stress reduction, or physiotherapy to reduce excessive loading forces on tooth roots.
Treat failing root-treated teeth before they develop chronic periapical abscesses that accelerate hypercementosis at the root tip.
Panoramic dental X-rays every 2–3 years in adults over 40 allow early detection of hypercementosis before extraction becomes necessary, giving time for surgical planning.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain good oral hygiene with twice-daily brushing and daily flossing to prevent the dental decay and gum disease that can trigger local periapical inflammation.
Wear a custom night guard if you grind your teeth, reducing the chronic overloading of root structures.
Attend all recommended follow-up dental radiograph appointments so any change in the hypercementotic root can be detected early.
If you have Paget disease, tell your dentist before any dental extractions so they can plan appropriately and request pre-operative imaging.

recommended: A calcium-adequate diet (dairy, leafy greens, fortified foods) to support normal bone and cementum metabolism alongside any medical treatment for Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism
Adequate vitamin D through sunlight exposure and dietary sources, essential for calcium absorption and bone mineral regulation
Plenty of water and a low-sugar diet to reduce dental caries risk and the periapical infection that drives localised hypercementosis
avoid: Excessive calcium supplementation without medical guidance in patients with hyperparathyroidism or Paget disease, where calcium metabolism is already disrupted
High-sugar and acidic foods that promote dental caries and subsequent periapical inflammation, which can initiate or worsen localised hypercementosis

exerciseGuidelines: There are no exercise restrictions specific to hypercementosis. For patients with Paget disease of bone, physical activity is beneficial for maintaining bone strength and mobility, but weight-bearing exercise should be guided by the physician in areas of active disease to avoid fracture. After surgical tooth extraction of a hypercementotic root, avoid strenuous exercise and contact sports for 3–5 days until the socket is healing well to reduce bleeding and swelling.
dailyManagement: Maintain meticulous oral hygiene to prevent new periapical infections from developing around remaining teeth.
Attend dental reviews as scheduled; asymptomatic hypercementosis does not require frequent visits beyond the normal recall interval.
If you have Paget disease: take bisphosphonate therapy as prescribed and attend follow-up ALP blood tests to confirm disease control.
Wear your occlusal splint nightly if prescribed for bruxism.
Notify your dentist of any new jaw pain, swelling, or loosening of teeth that might indicate a change in the hypercementotic root or the development of a periapical lesion.
prognosis: The prognosis for incidentally discovered asymptomatic hypercementosis is excellent. The condition is benign and static in most cases; it does not progress to malignancy and does not threaten tooth survival on its own. When associated with chronic periapical periodontitis, treatment of the underlying infection results in periapical healing while leaving the cementum excess in place as a harmless radiographic finding. The main clinical consequence is the increased surgical complexity of tooth extraction when it becomes necessary, which is managed safely with appropriate pre-operative planning. In patients with Paget disease, control of the underlying metabolic disorder stabilises jaw changes and reduces the risk of ankylosis progression, though some degree of root fusion to the surrounding bone may be permanent. Overall, hypercementosis is a radiographic diagnosis with minimal direct impact on quality of life when associated dental disease is treated promptly.
recoveryTimeline: Surgical extraction of a hypercementotic tooth follows the timeline of any surgical dental extraction: soft tissue healing takes 2–3 weeks, bone remodelling in the socket is largely complete by 6–8 weeks, and full bony healing takes 3–6 months. If the extraction site is intended for implant placement, a grafting and healing period of 4–6 months typically precedes implant placement. Root canal therapy for associated periapical pathology resolves symptoms within 2–7 days; radiographic evidence of periapical bone healing is seen at 6–12 months on follow-up X-ray.
complications: Extraction difficulty and root fracture — the principal clinical complication; attempting simple forceps extraction of a bulbous hypercementotic root risks fracturing the root tip or the surrounding alveolar bone, leaving a retained root that requires surgical retrieval.

Ankylosis — in severe hypercementosis, especially in Paget disease, the excess cementum may fuse to the surrounding bone, making extraction exceptionally difficult and potentially requiring partial bone removal.

Inferior alveolar nerve injury — when surgical extraction of a hypercementotic mandibular molar root is performed in close proximity to the inferior alveolar nerve, temporary or permanent lip numbness may result; pre-operative CBCT assessment significantly reduces this risk.

Missed underlying systemic disease — generalised hypercementosis identified on dental radiographs but not investigated may delay diagnosis of Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism, during which time systemic complications of those conditions may progress.

sources.title: Kramer IRH et al. Histological Typing of Odontogenic Tumours (WHO Classification, 2nd ed). 1992

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic apical periodontitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic apical periodontitis produces a periapical radiolucency (dark halo) around the root tip from bone destruction; hypercementosis produces radiopacity (whiteness) from excess tissue on the root itself, though both can coexist on the same tooth.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irreversible pulpitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulpitis causes throbbing spontaneous pain and temperature sensitivity from an inflamed living pulp; hypercementosis is asymptomatic and found on X-ray, though pulp death leading to periapical infection can cause both conditions to coexist on the same tooth.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperparathyroidism is a systemic endocrine disorder that may cause generalised hypercementosis as one feature alongside hypercalcaemia, bone resorption (brown tumours), and kidney stones; distinguishing between the dental finding and the underlying cause requires blood tests.

relatedConditions.name: Acute apical periodontitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute and chronic periapical periodontitis are the primary local triggers for reactive hypercementosis; treating the apical infection arrests further cementum deposition.

relatedConditions.name: Pulp degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Pulp degeneration predisposes to periapical inflammation, which is the local stimulus for localised hypercementosis; endodontic treatment of degenerative pulps removes this stimulus.

relatedConditions.name: Periodontosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe periodontal disease affecting the supporting structures around a tooth creates altered loading and inflammatory conditions at the root surface that can contribute to reactive cementum changes.

relatedConditions.name: Dental root caries
relatedConditions.relevance: Root caries can progress to pulp involvement and periapical infection, the principal local cause of reactive hypercementosis; prompt treatment of root caries prevents this progression.

faqs.question: What is hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: Hypercementosis is the excessive build-up of cementum — the tissue covering tooth roots — producing a rounded or bulbous root end visible on dental X-rays. It is almost always discovered incidentally, causes no symptoms, and does not require treatment unless the tooth needs extraction for other reasons.

faqs.question: What causes hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are chronic low-grade infection at the root tip (periapical periodontitis), excessive biting forces on a tooth, and systemic metabolic conditions such as Paget disease of bone and hyperparathyroidism. In many patients no clear cause is identified and it is considered an age-related variant.

faqs.question: Is hypercementosis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Hypercementosis itself is benign and rarely causes problems. Its main clinical impact is making tooth extraction more difficult when the bulbous root locks in the socket. When it affects many teeth in both jaws it signals a possible underlying condition such as Paget disease, which does warrant medical attention.

faqs.question: Does hypercementosis cause tooth pain?
faqs.answer: Hypercementosis itself is painless. However, it frequently develops in response to chronic periapical infection, which may cause mild root-tip tenderness or biting sensitivity. Treating the infection with root canal therapy resolves the pain even though the excess cementum remains.

faqs.question: How is hypercementosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Hypercementosis is diagnosed on dental radiographs — periapical or panoramic X-rays — where it appears as a rounded, bulbous, or thickened root end with a regular border. When it involves many teeth in both jaws, blood tests for alkaline phosphatase, calcium, and parathyroid hormone are needed to look for Paget disease or hyperparathyroidism.

faqs.question: Does hypercementosis require treatment?
faqs.answer: No treatment is needed for asymptomatic hypercementosis on an otherwise healthy tooth. If the tooth has associated periapical infection, root canal therapy is needed. If the tooth must be removed for any reason, surgical extraction is required because the bulbous root cannot be delivered by simple forceps methods.

faqs.question: Can hypercementosis be reversed?
faqs.answer: The excess cementum that has already formed does not resorb or reverse once deposited. Treating the underlying cause — such as performing root canal therapy for periapical infection or controlling Paget disease with bisphosphonates — can stop further deposition but will not shrink the root back to its original shape.

faqs.question: Is hypercementosis associated with Paget disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Paget disease of bone is the most important systemic cause of generalised hypercementosis, affecting 30–60% of patients with jaw involvement. Hypercementosis across multiple teeth in both jaws should prompt blood tests for elevated alkaline phosphatase, the primary marker of active Paget disease.

faqs.question: Why does hypercementosis make tooth extraction difficult?
faqs.answer: The normal root tapers toward the tip, allowing it to be delivered from the socket with rocking movements. A hypercementotic root has a rounded or bulbous apex wider than the root trunk, effectively wedging it in the bony socket. Forcing extraction can fracture the root or the jaw bone, so a surgical approach with bone removal is required.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypercementosis and a cementoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Both appear as increased density on dental X-rays, but a cementoblastoma is a true benign neoplasm producing a solid radiopaque mass fused to the root with a distinctive radiolucent rim, and it must be removed surgically. Hypercementosis is a reactive overgrowth without a tumour component, is usually asymptomatic, and does not require removal unless extraction is needed for other reasons.

faqs.question: How common is hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: Hypercementosis affects roughly 1–5% of the general adult population on dental X-ray surveys. It is much more common — about 30–60% — in patients with Paget disease of bone affecting the jaw, and prevalence increases with age in the general population.

faqs.question: Can root canal treatment cause hypercementosis to worsen?
faqs.answer: No. Root canal therapy treats the periapical infection that drives reactive hypercementosis. Removing the inflammatory stimulus stops further cementum deposition. After successful root canal treatment, the existing bulbous root remains but does not enlarge further.

faqs.question: What specialist treats hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: Most patients are managed by their general dentist through observation and radiographic monitoring. An endodontist treats any associated periapical infection with root canal therapy. An oral and maxillofacial surgeon handles surgical extraction when needed. When systemic disease is the cause, a rheumatologist or endocrinologist manages the underlying condition.

faqs.question: Can hypercementosis fuse teeth together?
faqs.answer: In a small number of cases, hypercementosis on adjacent teeth can cause their roots to join together via their cementum — a condition called concrescence. This is uncommon but significantly complicates extraction and any orthodontic movement of the fused teeth.

faqs.question: Do I need to tell my dentist about my Paget disease?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it is important. Your dentist needs to know about Paget disease before any tooth extraction so they can order pre-operative CT imaging, plan a surgical approach, and discuss the risk of more difficult extraction or potential bleeding from the highly vascular Paget-affected bone.

faqs.question: Can bruxism cause hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Heavy, habitual teeth grinding (bruxism) places excessive stress on tooth roots, stimulating the periodontal ligament and cementoblasts to lay down more cementum as an adaptive response. Managing bruxism with an occlusal night guard may reduce this stimulus.

faqs.question: Is hypercementosis inherited?
faqs.answer: Most hypercementosis is not directly inherited. Systemic causes such as Paget disease do have a genetic component — about 15–40% of Paget disease cases have a family history, and mutations in SQSTM1 are found in approximately 10% of cases. Hypercementosis associated with Paget disease therefore has an indirect hereditary dimension.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for generalised hypercementosis?
faqs.answer: The key tests are serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) to screen for Paget disease, serum calcium to check for hypercalcaemia, parathyroid hormone (PTH) to assess for hyperparathyroidism, and phosphate levels. Bone-specific ALP and urinary bone turnover markers provide more precise assessment if total ALP is elevated.

faqs.question: Can a tooth with hypercementosis have a dental implant placed after extraction?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided there is adequate bone. Surgical extraction of a hypercementotic tooth preserves surrounding bone more reliably than a traumatic forceps removal, so a careful surgical approach actually facilitates subsequent implant placement. A bone graft may be placed in the socket at extraction to maintain volume.

metaDescription: Hypercementosis is excess cementum on tooth roots seen on X-ray, linked to Paget disease and infection. Learn causes, diagnosis and extraction risks.
